author: 🇰🇼 Ibrahim Almaraghi
msg:
Not a legal expert myself 

But i do work somewhere where i get a good exposure to legal stuff in a daily manner with other experts, so what i am about to say is "logically driven":

If the project itself is setting a minimum split on their own platform, and NOT forcing any split outside of it (like putting penalties on the users if they do so outside the market place" then it "should" be fine, 

For example:

Scenario 1:
"We are putting a minimum split on the official marketplace."

Ok, that's fine.

Scenario 2:
"We are putting a minimum split on the official marketplace AND You are not allowed to trade outside our marketplace or you will be penalized for it"

That is not fine.

One does "still" have freedom of choice.

The other is pure manipulation.

A minimum split will only put "extra" efforts on the UNO side of things if this was implemented.

Again, I'm not a legal expert, and rules do/may differ from one country to another, but in a basic sense on legal matters, a minimum split "shouldn't be" illegal if imposed on project's own market place only.

author: Gwailow
msg:
I have a different viewpoint on this which is where my concerns stem from. 

The default, official (and currently only) marketplace is establishing a common financial arrangement for leases through the marketplace that limits the free choice of the potential ULO and limits competition amongst UNOs. For a regular person interested in becoming a ULO, their first port of call is likely to that marketplace as its directly available from the app - so its difficult to say that the ULO has any choice on the financial aspect of any lease they take through the marketplace.  Saying that UNOs can set their own terms is fine but this is looking at it from a UNO perspective - someone who just downloads the app and wants a license is unlikely to be in a position where they can contact a bunch of UNOs to get the best deal for them.

The danger here is that everyone is thinking from the perspective of a UNO and not from the potential ULO. It would be hard to say that setting a minimum does not limit competition or the freedom to choose from the ULO perspective. The one argument that I think could stand up is whether or not this is a financial arrangement but its a very grey area given that UPs are convertible to USD.

author: Gwailow
msg: Free market pricing may not be restricted but access to that range of pricing is restricted (or at least not readily and obviously available).  That for me is where the issue lies.

msg: Moreover, it is not accurate to claim that UNOs can create their own marketplace with the same functionality as the in-app one, due to the lack of public APIs that would allow UNOs to manage licenses in an equivalent way. For instance: they cannot automatically lease a license. Instead, they must manually generate an activation code, so it needs personnel 24/7. They are even unable to check whether a license is already leased, which prevents them from properly removing it from their own marketplace
